title: Dense Word Embeddings
concept: Dense embeddings compress words into small, continuous float vectors (e.g. 300 dimensions), packing meaning into a compact feature space.

exercises.prompt: What is the main goal of using dense word embeddings?

options.text: To compress word meanings into a small, continuous vector space
To list all words in alphabetical order
To check if a word is spelled correctly

exercises.explain: Dense embeddings compress the meaning of words into a small number of continuous decimal values (dimensions), making it easy for computers to process language.

cards.title: Fitting Language in a Pocket
cards.body: Imagine trying to store the meaning of every word in the dictionary. If you give each word its own unique dimension, you end up with millions of empty spaces. How do we compress the entire human vocabulary into a tiny, smart package that computers can actually read? The secret is dense word embeddings!

cards.title: What are Dense Embeddings?
cards.body: Dense word embeddings represent words as compact lists of numbers (usually 100 to 300 decimals). Because the list is small and full of actual numbers (not mostly zeros), it is called 'dense'. This compact structure allows computers to quickly compare words and find hidden relationships.

cards.title: The GPS Analogy
cards.body: Imagine a large city map. Instead of assigning a unique, giant street address to every single building, GPS coordinates pin every location using just two numbers: latitude and longitude. Similarly, dense embeddings pinpoint words on a coordinate grid of meaning using just a few hundred numbers instead of millions.

cards.title: Numbers with Meaning
cards.body: If we look at a dense embedding for the word 'Apple', it might look like: [0.25, -0.47, 0.81, ... 297 more numbers]. Because 'Orange' has a similar meaning, its numbers will be very close to Apple's: [0.27, -0.42, 0.79, ...]. This helps the computer instantly know they are both fruits!

cards.title: Is Smaller Worse?
cards.body: Many believe that because dense vectors are smaller, they lose crucial information about words. In reality, dense embeddings capture *more* subtle relationships (like analogies and synonyms) than giant, sparse representations, all while using a fraction of the computer's memory!

cards.title: Sparse vs. Dense
cards.body: Compare how sparse and dense embeddings store the word 'cat' in a computer's memory.

left.text: [0, 0, 0, 1, 0, 0 ... millions of zeros] -> Heavy, wastes space, doesn't show relationships.

exercises.prompt: What is the typical size (number of dimensions) of a dense word embedding vector?

options.text: One dimension for every word in the dictionary (millions)
A size that changes randomly every time we look at a word
Only one single dimension per word

exercises.explain: Dense embeddings typically use 100 to 300 dimensions to represent a word, which is incredibly small and fast compared to using the entire size of a dictionary.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Dense word embeddings are mostly filled with zeros, just like sparse vectors.
exercises.explain: Unlike sparse representations, dense embeddings store meaningful decimal values in every slot, so there are no wasted 'zero' slots.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe this compact way of representing text.
exercises.sentence: Instead of giant arrays of zeros, we use ________ embeddings to pack word meanings into a small, continuous list of numbers.
exercises.explain: Dense embeddings represent words using a compact, fixed list of decimal values, packing meaning into a small space.

exercises.prompt:
Two AI systems explain how they represent words. Spot the AI that is correctly explaining DENSE word embeddings.

Sentence A: "We assign a giant list of 50,000 numbers to each word, where only one position is a 1 and the rest are 0."
Sentence B: "We assign a compact list of 300 decimal coordinates to each word, placing similar words close to each other in space."
exercises.explain: Sentence A describes a sparse bag-of-words model, not a dense embedding. Sentence B shows a dense embedding, where coordinates are continuous floats (decimals) and similar words group together.

exercises.prompt: You are building a chat application and want to use dense word embeddings to understand user messages. Which of the following best describes what the word 'coffee' looks like when processed as a dense embedding?

options.text: A sparse vector using millions of coordinates to represent a single word.
A compact list of 300 floating-point numbers placing related words near each other.
A dictionary that matches words with their exact alphabetical page numbers.
A list containing only zeros and ones for every word in the dictionary.

exercises.explain: Dense embeddings use a small, fixed number of decimal values (dimensions) to represent words, allowing similar words to sit close to each other in vector space. Representing words with millions of zeros (sparse) is highly inefficient and misses relationships!
